ankai: Ankai matches have the highest retention risk — one-year departure rate is significantly above other pairings
Kai-party candidates face 'nothing else is bearable' — output starts declining at six months, potentially within the guarantee period
When an-party candidates burn out and leave, the client company will question the headhunter's recommendation quality
Bad year + ankai match = near-certain departure; headhunters should avoid recommending during this window
If recommendation is necessary, suggest project-based or consultant engagement to reduce risk for both sides
yusui: Sui-party candidate's high-energy output is unsustainable — strong initial performance does not guarantee long-term stability
Candidate's yu-party nature makes them prone to accepting below-market compensation; headhunter must protect their floor
After one year, fatigue accumulates — without a company feedback mechanism, departure intent rises sharply
Bad year + sui party = double drain; post-guarantee departure risk is elevated
kisei: Adjustment period may last longer than expected — company may want to replace the candidate within the first month
If headhunter fails to pre-set adjustment expectations, both sides will feel misled
Ki-party candidate's stress tolerance has limits — beyond that, growth turns to harm
mei: Homogeneous match won't deliver the 'fresh perspective' or 'transformation' the company expects
If the company is hiring to solve existing problems, a mei-position candidate can't help
Headhunter packaging 'zero friction' as 'bringing innovation' is the most common mis-recommendation
gyotai: Candidate's mission drive may lead them to accept below-market terms; headhunter must negotiate fair compensation
Not seeing concrete short-term returns is normal — pre-set expectations with the company that this is a long-term investment hire
